article.title: Artemis II Lifts Off Today as Four Astronauts Begin Humanity's First Crewed Lunar Voyage in Over 50 Years
article.summary: NASA's SLS rocket and Orion capsule are set to launch at 6:24 p.m. EDT from Kennedy Space Center, sending four astronauts on a 10-day lunar flyby that will break the Apollo 13 distance record.
article.body_markdown:
## Overview

After years of delays, hydrogen leaks, and a major program restructuring, NASA's Artemis II mission is poised to launch today from Kennedy Space Center, sending four astronauts on a 10-day voyage around the Moon. The countdown clock began Monday at 4:44 p.m. EDT, and the launch team [received official clearance](https://www.nasa.gov/blogs/missions/2026/04/01/live-artemis-ii-launch-day-updates/) for tanking operations at 7:33 a.m. this morning, with liftoff targeted for 6:24 p.m. EDT.

If successful, Artemis II will mark humanity's first crewed journey beyond low Earth orbit since Apollo 17 returned from the Moon in December 1972 — a gap of more than 53 years. The mission builds on the uncrewed Artemis I test flight of 2022 and follows [earlier reporting by The Machine Herald](/article/2026-02/05-nasa-delays-artemis-ii-crewed-lunar-mission-to-march-after-hydrogen-leak-during-fuel-test) on the hydrogen leak that pushed the launch from its original February date.

## The Crew

Four astronauts will fly aboard the Orion capsule, named "Integrity," according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/):

- **Commander Reid Wiseman** (NASA), a former Navy fighter pilot who spent 165 days aboard the International Space Station in 2014
- **Pilot Victor Glover** (NASA), a former Navy captain and test pilot who will become the first Black astronaut to fly beyond low Earth orbit
- **Mission Specialist Christina Koch** (NASA), who holds NASA's record for the longest single spaceflight by a woman at 328 days and participated in the first all-female spacewalk
- **Mission Specialist Jeremy Hansen** (Canadian Space Agency), a former Royal Canadian Air Force fighter pilot making his maiden spaceflight and becoming the first non-American to visit the lunar vicinity

## Mission Profile

The Space Launch System rocket, standing 322 feet tall and producing approximately 8.8 million pounds of thrust, will propel Orion through an eight-minute climb to orbit at roughly five miles per second, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). From there, the mission follows a carefully choreographed sequence:

- **Hours 0-3:** Low Earth orbit passes lasting approximately 90 minutes each, followed by an elliptical orbit reaching an apogee of 43,760 miles, with capsule separation from the propulsion stage at 3 hours 23 minutes
- **Hour 25:** A trans-lunar injection burn lasting 6 minutes and 5 seconds boosts the spacecraft's velocity by approximately 900 mph, initiating a four-day coast to the Moon
- **Lunar approach:** Orion will follow a "free return" trajectory, passing within 4,100 miles of the lunar surface without entering orbit. The crew will observe 21 percent of the Moon's far side in sunlight — a view never before seen by human eyes
- **Return and reentry:** The capsule will hit Earth's atmosphere at approximately 25,000 mph, endure temperatures of 5,000 degrees on its 16.5-foot heat shield, and deploy 11 parachutes in sequence before splashing down in the Pacific Ocean at 15-17 mph

The total journey spans approximately 685,000 miles over 9 to 10 days, with splashdown expected on April 10, as reported by [Live Science](https://www.livescience.com/space/live/artemis-ii-launch-wednesday-april-1).

## Breaking Distance Records

At its farthest point, Orion is expected to travel 252,000 miles from Earth, exceeding the Apollo 13 record of 248,655 miles by approximately 4,000 miles, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/). This will make the Artemis II crew the humans who have traveled farthest from Earth in history.

## What We Don't Know

- **Weather window:** The forecast holds at an 80 percent chance of favorable conditions, according to [CBS News](https://www.cbsnews.com/news/nasa-artemis-ii-moon-launch-astronauts-flight-plan/), but the two-hour launch window leaves limited margin for delays
- **Heat shield performance:** The Orion heat shield experienced unexpected charring during the Artemis I uncrewed reentry; its behavior during the faster crewed reentry remains a closely watched variable
- **Cascade effects:** A successful Artemis II is a prerequisite for Artemis III, now restructured as an orbital test flight in 2027 following [NASA's program overhaul](/article/2026-03/03-nasa-overhauls-artemis-program-adds-new-mission-and-pushes-moon-landing-to-2028) announced in March. Any anomaly could further delay the eventual crewed lunar landing, currently targeted for 2028

## Scientific Objectives

Beyond its role as a crewed test flight, Artemis II will carry the AVATAR investigation, which studies radiation and microgravity effects on human health using organ-chip technology, according to [NASA](https://www.nasa.gov/mission/artemis-ii/). The mission is designed to demonstrate the capabilities needed for sustained deep-space exploration, laying groundwork for eventual missions to the lunar surface and, eventually, Mars.

The launch represents the culmination of a program that has weathered budget overruns, technical setbacks, and the longest gap between crewed lunar missions in history. As of this morning, all systems remain on track for the 6:24 p.m. EDT liftoff.
